coredata.dc:title: Multiplex heteroatoms doped carbon nano dots with enhanced catalytic reduction of ionic dyes and QR code security label for anti-spurious applications
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, J. Vinoth
Kavitha, G.
Albasher, Gadah
Sajjad, Maryium
Arulmozhi, R.
Komal, M.
Nivetha, M. Sherlin
Abirami, N.
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a simple hydrothermal approach was used to make multiplex heteroatoms doped carbon dots from Tinospora cordifolia miers plant extract. Their ability to the catalytic activity of dyes and anti-spurious applications was evaluated. The formation of NBCNDs and source of (T. cordifolia miers) study the optical properties, and functional groups are investigated using UV–Visible spectroscopy and FT-IR techniques. The synthesized NBCNDs structure and elemental compositions were examined via HR-TEM, XRD, and XPS, respectively. According to the HRTEM images, the average particle size of the NBCNDs was around 4.3± 1 nm, with d-spacing of 0.19 nm. The obtained NBCNDs were exposed under 395 nm UV light to emit bluish-green tuneable fluorescence with QY (quantum yield) of 23.7%. The prepared NBCNDs as a potential catalyst for the AYR and CV dye reduction process using freshly prepared NaBH4, with determined rate constant values at 0.1220 and 0.1521 min−1, respectively. Lastly, we constructed a quick response (QR) code security label for anti-spurious applications using stencil techniques. The “confidential info” was encrypted using a QR code digital system, and the decryption was read using a smartphone under 365 nm light irradiation.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a simple hydrothermal approach was used to make multiplex heteroatoms doped carbon dots from Tinospora cordifolia miers plant extract. Their ability to the catalytic activity of dyes and anti-spurious applications was evaluated. The formation of NBCNDs and source of (T. cordifolia miers) study the optical properties, and functional groups are investigated using UV–Visible spectroscopy and FT-IR techniques. The synthesized NBCNDs structure and elemental compositions were examined via HR-TEM, XRD, and XPS, respectively. According to the HRTEM images, the average particle size of the NBCNDs was around 4.3± 1 nm, with d-spacing of 0.19 nm. The obtained NBCNDs were exposed under 395 nm UV light to emit bluish-green tuneable fluorescence with QY (quantum yield) of 23.7%. The prepared NBCNDs as a potential catalyst for the AYR and CV dye reduction process using freshly prepared NaBH4, with determined rate constant values at 0.1220 and 0.1521 min−1, respectively. Lastly, we constructed a quick response (QR) code security label for anti-spurious applications using stencil techniques. The “confidential info” was encrypted using a QR code digital system, and the decryption was read using a smartphone under 365 nm light irradiation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the current decade, Carbon Dots (CDs) are a novel family member of carbon-contain groups, zero-dimensional (0D) fluorescent particles of extremely tiny size below 10 nm. Carbon nanoparticles with fluorescence were accidently discovered during the re-purification of SWCNTs (single-walled carbon nanotubes) in 2004. The centre core of CDs is in a sp2-hybridized state, they have a high percentage of carbonyl, hydroxyl, epoxy, oxygen, amino, hydrogen, and some elements, as well as the advantages of less cost raw resources and simple production techniques (Yang et al., 2020). When compared to similar fluorescent-based materials such as semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) (CdSe, CdTe, Si, Zn) and black phosphorus quantum dots, the fluorescent CDs have unique characteristics such as quick synthesis, small size, interesting optical properties, high aqueous solubility, photobleaching resistance, tunable emission and excitation spectra, chemical inertness, good biocompatibility, and environmental-safety (Xu et al., 2020). Valcárcel et al. examined CDs scientifically and rationally such as origin type, quantum confinement, and morphology structure. Depending on their distribution polymer quantum dots (PQDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), and metal quantum dots. As a result, CNDs are widely used in photocatalyst, nanomedicine, dye-sensitized solar cells, chemical sensors, hydrogen production, electrocatalysis, carbon capture, etc., (Vinoth Kumar et al., 2021). Top-down and bottom-up routes are mainly used to synthesize CNDs. Large carbon nanomaterials (carbon nanotubes and graphene sheets) are converted into tiny-sized fluorescent carbon nanoparticles by top-down processes such as chemical oxidation, arc discharge, electrochemical, and laser ablation. Bottom-up approaches produce CNDs by carbonizing small precursors through hydrothermal treatment, pyrolysis, and microwave technique (Chellasamy et al., 2022).

The hydrothermal approach is one of the most extensively used procedures because of its quick surface tuning and controlled CNDs morphology when compared to other techniques. Natural-derived carbon nanostructures have lately emerged as a new subject in green chemistry with a wide range of benefits. The nanomaterials preparation, carbon sources might be easily available from natural resources, inexpensive, and environmentally friendly (Mahesh et al., 2022). The following are some of the documented natural biomass carbon precursors: prickly pear cactus (Radhakrishnan and Panneerselvam, 2018), banana plant (Vandarkuzhali et al., 2017), Cissus quadrangularis (Murugan et al., 2021), Aloe barbadensis Miller (Malavika et al., 2021), and Pineapple peel (Vandarkuzhali et al., 2018), etc. Doping with different heteroatoms is an efficient way to alter the characteristics of nanoscale materials. To enhance the optical properties of CNDs, hetero-atoms like N (Nitrogen), O (Oxygen), B (Boron), P (Phosphorus), S (Sulfur), and Cl (Chlorine) have all been identified and synthesized (Liu et al., 2017a).

Nitrogen and boron seem to become the most effective dopant among the other hetero atom dopants because it improves the fluorescence quantum yield, structural properties, and surface morphology of the CNDs (Dong et al., 2013). Boron and nitrogen have been utilized to modify the electronic band structure of carbon to give multiple active surface sites and to increase catalytic properties synergistically. Due to the synergistic effect between the doped heteroatoms in CNDs, co-doping multiple heteroatoms on carbon dots has started to attract greater attention since it can result in a distinctive electronic structure. The multiplex heteroatoms doped carbon nanodots (M-CNDs) have attracted considerable attention. Hence the CNDs with boron (B) and nitrogen (N) work synergistically to increase catalytic properties (Chen et al., 2018; Cao et al., 2020). Zhu et al., synthesized N-doped CNDs with blue luminescence and a strong QY of up to 80% (Zhu et al., 2013). Wang et al. developed nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped CNDs with high green fluorescence (Wang et al., 2014). In comparison to nitrogen, sulfur, or phosphorus-doped CNDs, boron-doped CNDs have been rarely reported. Because nitrogen and boron co-doped CNDs have not been studied, it is still worthwhile to investigate for other applications (Shen and Xia, 2014; Ye et al., 2016).

The Tinospora cordifolia miers (T. cordifolia miers), also known as Guduchi is a huge, glabrous, widely spreading, perennial deciduous shrub found throughout India plains, it belongs to the Menispermaceae family (Jayaseelan et al., 2011). This plant extract acts as a carbon precursor. The plant is mainly used in historical ayurveda medicine and has a variety of therapeutic benefits including skin illnesses, inflammation, rheumatism, jaundice, radioprotective properties, diabetes, anti-periodic, allergic condition, urinary disorders, and so on. Various phytochemical components are present in T. cordifolia miers such as phenolics, polysaccharides, alkaloids, steroids, flavonoids, glycosides, and aliphatic compounds (Sharma et al., 2019; Asuntha et al., 2010). Nowadays, the increasing dyeing, textile, pharmaceutical, plastics, and other industries, the dumping raw sewage is being mixed into rivers and the ocean daily, which is extremely dangerous to people as well as marine aquatic animals. As a result, it is now vital to treat organic dye-containing industrial effluent with current methods. Some of the techniques used to remove hazardous pollutants from wastewaters are Fenton oxidation, membrane separation, oxidation, and photocatalytic degradation (Sodhani et al., 2022; Devi et al., 2022; Krishnan et al., 2021). At this time, it is critical to remove this hazardous pollution from the environment via current technology. In spite of this another global issue is counterfeiting which causes serious consequences for government organizations, businesses, education, and users. During the last few decades, counterfeits have been discovered everywhere, including fast-moving merchandise, microsystems, automobile components, pharmaceuticals, and official documents (Andres et al., 2014). The CNDs prove to be very beneficial as anti-counterfeiting agents due to their strong fluorescence characteristics, high quantum yields, minimal cytotoxicity, and numerous sources. To our knowledge, no studies on the anticounterfeiting applications of NBCNDs produced from T. cordifolia miers extract as a fluorescent material that has been reported (Kumar et al., 2021).

Here, we have constructed, low-cost N/B co-doped carbon nanodots (NBCNDs) using the hydrothermal process of T. cordifolia miers as carbon source, aqueous ammonia for nitrogen, and boric acid for boron sources respectively. The prepared NBCNDs well characterized with the help of UV–vis, FT-IR, TEM, XPS, Raman, and fluorescence emission spectrum were studied. The catalytic ability of the synthesized NBCNDs was investigated for the reduction of Alizarin yellow (AYR) and Crystal violet (CV) dyes using sodium borohydride (NaBH4). The synthesized NBCNDs have strong fluorescence nature and were used to construct QR code patterns on the model banknotes using fluorescent ink (FL ink) for anti-spurious applications.

The leaf of the T. cordifolia miers was collected from Paranur Village, Chengalpattu District, Tamil Nadu, and India. Boric acid (H3BO3), Aqueous ammonia (NH₄OH), Alizarin yellow (C13H9N3O5), Methylene blue (C16H18ClN3S), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), and polyvinyl alcohol ((C2H4O) x) was bought from Sigma-Aldrich. All reagents and chemicals laboratory grade (LR) were used without any pre-treatment. Double distilled water (DDW) was utilized to make solutions and other tests throughout the investigation.

The NBCNDs were synthesized through hydrothermal treatment. In a typical procedure, 46 mL of T. cordifolia miers plant extract, 2 mL of aq. NH3 and 2 mL of boric acid solution (2 M) which were transferred to 100 mL polymer-coated autoclave were placed in an oven heated at 200 °C for 12 h. The reaction is completed by carefully opening the autoclave once the temperature is reduced. To eliminate large particles the solution was filtered with 0.22 μm syringe filters. To eliminate unreacted molecules, the final product underwent dialysis against DW (distilled water) for 24 h using a membrane (MWCO 1000 Da). Finally, the obtained NBCNDs in their brown color solution were stored at 4 °C for further processes. The self-passivated carbon nanodots were synthesized in the above-mentioned procedure, but without the use of aq. ammonia and boric acid. Finally, the self-passivated carbon nanodots were obtained its mentioned name as undoped carbon nanodots (UDCNDs) kept at 5 °C for further studies.

The prepared NBCNDs were fully characterized by various analytical spectroscopic methods. To study the optical characteristics of synthesized NBCNDs UV–Vis (Shimadzu UV Spectrophotometer) and fluorescence spectra (Jasco FP 8500 spectrofluorometer) were used. Thermo Fisher Nicolet iS50 FTIR Spectrometer is used to examine the functional groups. The powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) analysis was conducted by Pan analytical Xpert Pro with a Cu Kr (1.5406 Å) with a scan range of 10–90 (2Ɵ). Raman spectrometer for NBCNDs was recorded by STR 500 nm laser. To study the structural morphology of prepared NBCNDs using a carbon-coated grid (200 mesh) for High Resolution-TEM (JOEL JEM 2100) microscope with a voltage of 200 kV and the particle size was measured using ImageJ software. The surface layers of the sample were examined to study the chemical composition by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS-PH15000 probe III) and the Casa-XPS tool was used to deconvolve the elements of the full survey spectrum.

The fluorescence quantum yield (FL-QY) measurement has been determined for nitrogen, boron co-doped NBCNDs, and using a 0.1 M H2SO4 aqueous solution of quinine sulfate it has a quantum yield of 0.54% at excitation wavelength 370 nm. The (FL-QY) of synthesized NBCNDs is calculated by the formula below:

Were, Q

x are the QY of the quinine sulfate and synthesized NBCNDs. The refractive indices of H2SO4 and H2O are ηstd 2 and η
x
2 , and the integrated emission regions are I
x and Istd, respectively. The Astd and A x are the optical densities of the quinine sulfate reference dye and NBCNDs.

The catalytic ability of the prepared NBCNDs was evaluated on the reduction of AYR, and CV dyes with the help of sodium borohydride (NaBH4). The optimal concentration of reactants (AYR, CV & NaBH4) and catalyst (NBCNDs) was determined through an optimal experiment. During the first reaction, 1 mL of each dye (1 × 10−5 M) was taken in a 3.5 mL cuvette, and 0.30 mL freshly prepared NaBH4 (0.50 M) in ice-cooled condition was added to the dyes. In the quartz cuvette, the reaction mixture volume was made up to 3 mL using water. At regular time intervals, this catalytic reduction process was observed. In the second step, prepared NBCNDs as nanocatalyst was introduced to the above-mentioned reaction. Finally, this reduction process was examined using UV–Vis spectroscopy linear time intervals.

The textile effluent were collected from textile industry situated at Chengalpattu, Tamil Nadu, and India. The samples of textile effluent were filtered via an 11 μm membrane, and then used for further treatment. In the systematic experiment, 3 mL of effluent was treated with NaBH4 (without catalyst), and no color change was observed. The NBCNDs catalyst was added in 50 μL to the textile effluent solution mentioned above. The absorbance intensity of the samples were measured using the UV–Vis spectrometer.

To synthesize the anti-spurious security ink, initially, 10 mL of 10% polyvinyl alcohol was taken and the prepared NBCNDs solution was added dropwise, the reaction was treated with ultrasonication for 15 min. This solution was extensively stirred for 30 min in a magnetic stirrer, generating viscous fluorescent ink. The synthesized FL ink was put into a blank refill tube, and pictures were written or drawn on regular filter paper the ink was then dried at room temperature in the open area. The dried paper was studied with UV light (365 nm), and the letters are showing were captured in a digital camera.

The fluorescent ink was carefully applied to the specific site on the model banknotes and was used with a stencil printing method. To start, a digital QR code image is encoded with “confidential info.” Was made using freely accessible software. The stencil was created by en-carving the appropriate sketch into a polyester sheet with a thickness of 100 μm using an electric cutter. The FL ink flow over the stencil frame with a roller makes printing the QR code pattern on the model bank notes easier, and it dries in 45 min at 50 °C.

The NBCNDs were synthesized through a hydrothermal process using selected precursor and dopants. After the extract had been carbonized, the color of the T. cordifolia. Miers plant extract changed from pale green to blackish brown, confirming the development of carbon nanodots (Arul and Sethuraman, 2018). Temperature and reaction time is a critical parameter in the formation of fluorescent NBCNDs with specified properties. The final filtrated solution containing the fluorescent NBCNDs was stored in a freezer at temperatures less than 4 °C for future usage.

The UV–Vis absorption and FL spectrum were used to determine the optical characteristics of NBCNDs. The UV absorption spectra clearly show absorbance peak centered at 273 nm and 318 nm. The first peak, at 273 nm obtained due to the aromatic carbon CC (sp2) bond for π-π* transitions. Whereas the second absorption peak at 318 nm is attributed to CO/CN bond for n- π* transitions as shown in Fig. 1 (a) (Atchudan et al., 2020). The absorption spectrum is greatly influenced by the size, solvent medium, physical and chemical environment. It has also been revealed that as the size of the particle decreases, the absorption spectrum broadens and intensity of the peaks will be less (Das et al., 2010). Fluorescence emission might be caused by the quantum size (Yan et al., 2010), defect (Zhu et al., 2012), electronic conjugate structures (Pan et al., 2010), and zig-zag sites (Eda et al., 2010). Fig. 1(b) shows the FL excitation spectra of the NBCNDs, with the maximum excitation wavelength found at 320 nm. The strong FL of NBCNDs is confirmed by the high-intensity excitation peak.

The FL emission spectrum of the prepared NBCNDs is shown in Fig. 2(a) with various excitation wavelengths ranges from 310 nm to 410 nm. The FL intensity was linearly increased owing to the different excitation wavelengths. This type of transition occurs in the emission spectrum owing to π-π* of the core of graphitic carbon. As the excitation dependant wavelength was increased from 330 to 400 nm, the FL intensity decreased which might be due to the n- π * transition due to the nitrogen functionalization and having non-bonded electrons. In the maximum excitation (λmax
(ext)) wavelength of 320 nm, the maximum emission (λmax
(em)) of 403 nm was observed. The excitation dependant emission behaviors of the prepared NBCNDs is because of the different sizes of NBCNDs. The synthesized NBCNDs have been broadly exploited in sensor and biological applications due to their unique fluorescence properties (Bhattacharya et al., 2015). The quantum yield (QY) of the fluorescent NBCNDs was found to be 23.7%. This unique fluorescence behavior of NBCNDs might be owing to anti-Stokes shift (λmax
(ext) > λmax
(em)) Fig. 2 (b) in which two, or extra photons are evenly absorbed by the surface of carbon nanodots, and the resulting light is emitted (Atchudan et al., 2019).

The XRD was used to study the structure, phase, and degree of crystallinity of the NBCNDs and UDCNDs as depicted in Fig. 3 (a). The synthesized NBCNDs had three dominant peaks at around 20.7°, 40.8°, and 45.5°, which corresponded to graphite lattice spacing C (002), (100), and (101) respectively (Atchudan et al., 2019). In general, the XRD peak for graphitic carbon occurs in the C (002) plane at 26° (2θ). In this current study, the C (002) plane at 2θ = 20.7° possesses a moderate amorphous nature owing to the surface functionalization of dopants boron (B) and nitrogen (N) in CNDs. The diffraction C (002), and C (101) planes of UDCNDS as-synthesized revealed at 2θ = 21.9°, 42.3° confirming the undoped carbon nanodots having amorphous nature. Moreover, the XRD study indicates that the NBCNDs and UDCNDs exhibit a moderate graphitic carbon structure. The interplanar distance (d-spacing value) for the synthesized NBCNDs was calculated using Bragg's equation (d = nλ/2 Sinθ), Where λ is X-rays wavelength (1.54 Å), n is a positive integer (1), and θ represents the position of the plane. The synthesized NBCNDs had d-spacing values of approximately 0.43 nm for (002) and 0.19 nm for (101) peaks. These results may be attributed to the calculated d-spacing values obtained from HR-TEM lattice fringe (Fig. 5(c)). The obtained results demonstrated the amorphous character is owing to closely filled carbon atoms by alkyl chains (Atchudan et al., 2015).

The technique of Fourier Transform-IR spectroscopy has been widely used to detect functional groups of the synthesized NBCNDs and UDCNDs materials as presented in Fig. 3(b). The spectrum of NBCNDs showed stretching frequencies at 1324, 1610, and 1020 cm−1 which ascribed to C–N, CO, B–O–C, and an epoxy ring respectively (Atchudan et al., 2018). The broad band appeared at 3374 and 3034 cm−1 is assigned to the generation of –OH/−NH groups and –CH stretching vibrations (Saini et al., 2021). The CO stretching vibrations at 1610 cm−1 shows sp2 hybridized carbon and honeycomb structure existing in the NBCNDs core. The B–O–C stretching vibrations band at 1020 cm−1 revealed that the boron atoms were successfully doped on the CNDs surface. These investigations identified the main functional groups that could endow the capacity of NBCNDs for rapid electron transport. These characteristic results that the presence of C, N, B, and O bonds, raising the electron density and mobility in NBCNDs (Beker et al., 2022). The comparison, of the UDCNDs spectrum, shows the peaks around 3000–3400 cm−1 were broad which can be due to the overlap of –OH/–NH and C–H stretching bands. The results of the FT-IR spectrum confirmed the hydroxyl, amine, carbonyl moieties, and carboxylic acid in the synthesized NBCNDs (Song et al., 2016).

The Raman spectrum of the synthesized NBCNDs and UDCNDs is shown in Fig. 4 (a). The spectrum exhibits two different Raman signals at around 1441, 1412, and 1626, 1623 cm−1, referred to as the D (defect region) reveal sp3 hybridization of carbon atoms and G (graphitic region) bands, founds that the carbon atoms is sp2 hybridized. The intensities of I
D and I
G ratio might be less than one it could be perfect graphite nature. The I
D and I
G ratio values were obtained to be 0.78, and 0.94 for NB-CNDs, and UDCNDs, and the intensity of I
D is less than I
G intensity (I
D < I
G ). This shows the synthesized NBCNDs mild graphite structure and proves the existence of moderate defects on the surface in the graphitic structure of NBCNDs when compared to UDCNDs with a slight increase in the I
D
/I
G values (Chandrasekaran et al., 2020). In Fig. 4(b) TGA (thermal analysis) was utilized to measure the thermal stability of NBCNDs and UDCNDs. First, 13% weight loss was found owing to the disintegration of the physically adsorbed moisture contents. In the 250°C–300 °C range, consequent decompose occurred it is attributed to the breakdown of O2 moieties for UDCNDs. Further, a subsequent weight loss was attained from 350 to 500 °C, due to the desorption of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. A 50% weight loss was found when the increasing temperature up to 600 °C, which may be related to the removal of surface functional groups from the carbon backbone of the UDCNDs. At temperatures up to 600 °C, minimal weight loss occurred and the full stability of NBCNDs is observed (Wang et al., 2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and surface state of NBCNDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and crystallinity characteristics of the prepared NBCNDs were observed using HRTEM images with SAED pattern. The synthesized NBCNDs are uniformly distributed from one another on the carbon-coated Cu grid and have a spherical shape, as shown in Fig. 5(a). The NBCNDs show a mean particle size of 4.3 ± 1 nm (ranging from 1.0 to 9 nm) and were determined to be spherical with a narrow size distribution Fig. 5(b). Lattice fringes were visible in the HRTEM images and value of d-spacing is 0.19 nm (Fig. 5(c)), which indicates the graphitic carbon (101) plane (Tang et al., 2021). Fig. 5(d) displays the selected area electron diffraction pattern (SAED) of NBCNDs which revealed the amorphous nature of carbon by the clear circle formation. The energy dispersive spectrum (EDS) of NBCNDs is shown in Fig. 5(e), and it may confirm the composition of various elements of the synthesized NBCNDs.

The XPS was utilized to determine the elemental composition percentage and bonding state. The full survey scan of NBCNDs shows the peaks at 283.9, 400.7, 190.9, and 531.8 eV ascribed to carbon (C1s), Nitrogen (N1s), Boron (B1s), and Oxygen (O 1s), respectively, as displayed in Fig. 6 (a). The bar graph Fig. 6(b) suggests atomic weight percentage (%) of the elements in NBCNDs were C (42.8%), N (10.3%), B (12.7), and O (34.2%), respectively. The XPS spectrum of Carbon (1s) has been deconvoluted into two major peaks at 284.7, and 285.9 eV, which correspond to the C–N/C–O, C–C functional groups over the surface of NBCNDs as shown in Fig. 6(c) (Beker et al., 2020). The C–N–C (pyridinic nitrogen) and C3–N (graphitic nitrogen) are the two components of the N 1s peak in Fig. 6(d), which appear at 400.2 and 402.3 eV, respectively. The C–N–C bond intensity in the NBCNDs spectrum was found to have significantly increased. Interestingly, nitrogen functionalities may create more active sites and increased defects over the catalysts (An et al., 2021). As a result, we can confirm that N has been effectively doped into graphite nanostructure frameworks (Liu et al., 2017b). The B1s spectrum is in the form of two peaks with binding energies 190.9 and 192.8 Fig. 6(e) which correspond to BC2O, B–O bond, and their proportions in the graphitic carbon layers. Fig. 6(f) depicts the high-resolution spectrum of O 1s, two fitted peaks at 532.2 eV, and 531.1 can be ascribed to the presence of C–OH/C–O–C and CO groups respectively as in Qian et al. (2014).

In recent years, CNDs doped with hetero-atoms have been widely studied in environmental applications. The synthesized NBCNDS catalysts might be used as an effective catalytic reduction for a wide range of dyes. The catalytic reduction of UDCNDs results is given in electronic supplementary information (ESI. 1). Most of the researchers used metal-doped nanoparticles (M-NPs) such as Sn, Ag, Fe, Zn, Ce, Pd and others have been used as catalysts in ET (electron transfer) reactions using reducing agents. In this condition, a metal-free and eco-friendly catalyst (NBCNDs) is used in the reduction of dyes. The optimum concentrations of NaBH4, dyes and NBCNDs were found by numerous experiments (Vinoth kumar et al., 2022).

The Alizarin yellow R (AYR) dye catalytic reduction was observed with the support of a UV spectrometer in the aqueous medium. The (AYR) dye mixed with water has a high carcinogenic substance, organic content, and a high level of toxicity. The AYR dye has a salicylic acid derivative, belongs to the azo dye family, and is mainly used as an indicator, biological stain, and in the dyeing industry. Owing to its complex molecule, aromatic nature, and its non-biodegradability, it creates skin diseases, cancer, and neurotoxicity problems in humans (Govarthanan et al., 2021). The UV–visible absorption peak Fig. 7 (a) shows the AYR dye reduction by NaBH4 (freshly prepared ice-cooled conditioned) without NBCNDs catalyst, there is no considerable reduction, which was observed with the regular period up to 120 min. In Fig. 7(b) the synthesized NBCNDs (50 μL) were introduced to the reaction mixture which contains AYR and NaBH4 mixture, and the AYR dye intensity was considerably reduced which was confirmed by UV–Vis spectroscopy. Finally, the AYR dye reduction is completed within 20 min owing to the presence of (NBCNDs) catalyst. The present results proved that the synthesized NBCNDs remarkable catalytic behavior increased the rate of AYR reduction using sodium borohydride (NaBH4). This can be achieved by reducing the activation energy of the process by using NBCNDs (Dai et al., 2015). Using the equation of pseudo-first-order kinetics, we calculated the rate constant of (k) value for the AYR dye reduction.
ln
(
A t
/A 0
)
= - kt

At/A0 are the absorbance (at 0) and various times, the plot of ln (At/A0) Vs t (time) has obtained a good linearity plot. The linear plot was used to obtain the coefficient (R2), slope (S), and intercept (k) values as depicted in Fig. 7(c). The reduction experiments were conducted independently three times, and the results are displayed in the form of a linearity plot which was achieved between absorbance and time taken. The R2 and catalytic rate constant (Kcat) values were determined to be 0.1220 min−1 and 0.9784 respectively. To identify the reduction by-products of the AYR dye with the support of GC-MS (Gas chromatography-mass spectrometry), the results of the (m/z) value of the fresh AYR structure is 287.23 as displayed in Fig. 7(d). The reduction product with m/z values of 138.12 and 153.14 is shown in Fig. 7(e), which is very much lower than pure AYR m/z values. These results clearly show that the AYR dye was reduced, and results in the formation of p-nitroaniline and 5-aminosalicylic acid as reduction products (Dhanush and Sethuraman, 2021).

The catalytic reduction pathways occur in the combination of dyes, NaBH4 acts as a reducing agent, and NBCNDs. The Langmuir-Hinshelwood mechanism can clearly explain this catalytic reduction mechanism and the schematic representation was depicted in Fig. 9 . The interaction between the dye molecules and NaBH4 was a charge transfer (CT) mechanism, where both dye molecules served as e− acceptors and NaBH4 as an electron donor. Only dyes and NaBH4 reduction reaction were kinetically controlled owing to the significant potential difference. Dyes and borohydride (BH4 −) ions were mutually adsorbed on the NBCNDs surface and act as a surface mediator for the electron transfer from the borohydride ions to dyes. The energy barrier between the reactant as well as the product was reduced as a result the performance of NBCNDs surface mediator, and the reduction reaction was accelerated by the generation of hydrogen gas. The electron density on the NBCNDs surface is enhanced with the help of NBCNDs, due to sharing of electrons. Thus, the NBCNDs surface states are most effective due to electrons transfer from BH4 − to dyes. The energy gap between the reactant and the products was decreased, resulting in lower activation energy and a higher reaction rate of dyes. The NBCNDs as a remarkable catalyst due to their high strong electron absorption capacity and huge surface area. NBCNDs obtained from green sources are an effective catalytic activity as given in Table 1 . It has been found that carbon nano dots co-doped with nitrogen and boron exhibit enhanced catalytic activity for the degradation of two different kinds of ionic dyes. When compared to other publications, the reduction processes occur earlier, and this indicates improved reduction activity. On the other hand, the above-mentioned characteristics, are absent in UDCNDs, but only the bare carbon sites contribute to the dye reduction. Owing to the reasons stated above, N/B doped CNDs might provide effective electron transport (Zhou et al., 2017; Li et al., 2019).

Counterfeiting is a global issue that poses a significant challenge to governments, corporations, and consumers. In this instance, anti-spurious agents must be developed that make it simple to detect fake currency, official papers, certifications, and passports. The synthesized NBCNDs contain effective fluorescent properties, which are very supportive for anti-spurious applications in the real world. The following materials employed in this application are mainly used in semiconductor quantum dots, rare-earth metals, inorganic perovskites, up-conversion nanoparticles, and polymer dots (Rival et al., 2021). To start, the prepared fluorescent ink (FLI) was used to draw “Letters” on widely available normal paper and dried at room temperature. The material written on paper is invisible to the naked eye Fig. 10 (a) but under UV irradiation, “Letters” glow due to the fluorescent nature of NBCNDs Fig. 10(b) (Dhanush and Sethuraman, 2021). As a result of this study, the prepared NBCNDs were successfully used as a potential anti-spurious agent. To examine the stability of prepared NBCNDs FLI, we have recorded the fluorescence spectrum and the fluorescent ink was irradiated at UV light (365 nm) at regular time intervals as shown in Fig. 10 (c). Initially, without UV light the NBCNDs ink decreased within 25 min and remain constant, and it confirmed unstable from the plot of (I). The NBCNDs ink under UV light (365 nm) even upto 2 h it is stable which is confirmed from the plot of (II). With the excellent photostability, ease available of raw materials, and simple preparation method, we have selected NBCNDs FLI as the effective material for the advancement of fluorescent ink. To move on to the upcoming generation level, the synthesized NBCNDs FLI will be used in printing technology. Encoded and decoded methods using digital Quick response (QR) codes will be dependable in the contemporary world for keeping hidden information such as authentication, phone number, address, personal and financial details, and so on. The encrypted data file is to be accessible using a digital QR scanner application. We may use the model banknote to print a QR code label on the banknote, and Fig. 11 (a) shows a schematic depiction of stencil printing operations. Under UV irradiation, the encoded information on printed banknotes is visible, and the decryption mechanism is occurred using a smartphone, due to the strong fluorescence nature of NBCNDs the photographic images are shown in Fig. 11(b). Lastly, we have checked the stability of printed banknotes for further study. It was examined like crushing, folding, and wetting study. The FLI printed on the QR code label is stable after many multiple folds and also, we tested the wetting durability by soaking it in ordinary DI water. The data decoded mechanism was successful after several folding and soaking tests as shown by the photographic image conformation (ESI 3). The excellent photostability, mechanical folding assay, and water durability of the printed QR code reveal that NBCNDs FLI may be developed as next-level security codes (Rival et al., 2021).

In summary, we synthesized multiplex heteroatom-doped carbon nanodots utilising a straightforward hydrothermal method. The plant T. cordifolia miers extract, ammonia solution, and boric acid act as a source for carbon, nitrogen, and boron respectively. The XPS and FT-IR results reveal that the surface of carbon nanodots (CNDs) has been functionalized with the multiplex heteroatoms (Nitrogen and Boron). The synthesized NBCNDs exhibit spherical morphology with an average particle size of 4.3 nm. The XRD pattern results affirm the amorphous and graphitic nature of the synthesized NBCNDs. The superior optical properties of the NBCNDs were explained by the results of Fluorescence and UV–Vis spectroscopy. The catalytic efficiency of synthesized NBCNDs was examined through the reduction of cationic (AYR) and anionic dyes (CV) using NaBH4. This finding indicates that the NBCNDs enhance the surface states and catalytic activity. The obtained NBCNDs can be successfully applied to FL ink for anti-spurious purposes and are inexpensive and non-toxic. Additionally, the stencil printing method was used to develop a smartphone-readable QR code on a banknote. Overall findings point the NBCNDs as a viable fluorescence probe for real-world applications.

Arulmozhi: Visualization. M. Komal: Data curation. M. Sherlin Nivetha: Validation. N. Abirami: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.
